location_name: Wounded Knee Creek, Pine Ridge, South Dakota
main_actor: United States 7th Cavalry Regiment under Colonel James Forsyth
description: On 29 December 1890 the US 7th Cavalry surrounded a Lakota band of Big Foot (Spotted Elk) returning from Standing Rock to Pine Ridge. During disarmament, firing began; the cavalry deployed four Hotchkiss guns against the encampment. Between 250 and 300 Lakota were killed, of whom roughly half were women and children. Twenty US soldiers received Medals of Honor for the engagement; these have not been rescinded. The event is conventionally treated as the symbolic close of the US Army's Indian Wars campaign of conquest, though further actions continued.
casualties_source: Utley, R. (1963). The Last Days of the Sioux Nation; Jensen et al. (1991)
ethical_notes: Referred to as 'battle' in contemporary US sources and on some surviving monuments; US Army terminology officially reclassified as 'massacre' only progressively in the twentieth century. Forsyth was initially relieved of command by General Miles but reinstated after a court of inquiry.

sources.citation: Utley, R.M. (1963). The Last Days of the Sioux Nation. Yale University Press.
Jensen, R.E., Paul, R.E., & Carter, J.E. (1991). Eyewitness at Wounded Knee. University of Nebraska Press.

main_actor: English East India Company force under Robert Clive
description: On 23 June 1757 a small Company force under Robert Clive defeated the army of Nawab Siraj-ud-Daulah of Bengal, largely through prior subornation of the Nawab's commander Mir Jafar. The outcome transferred de facto political and fiscal authority over Bengal — the wealthiest province of the Mughal Empire — to the East India Company, initiating Company rule on the Indian subcontinent. The engagement was militarily minor but politically decisive.
casualties_source: Dalrymple, W. (2019). The Anarchy (Bengali casualties ~500; Company ~22 killed)
ethical_notes: Classified CONQUEST rather than BATTLE because the effective outcome was transfer of sovereignty; the word 'battle' is standard English-language usage but understates the political character.

sources.citation: Dalrymple, W. (2019). The Anarchy: The Relentless Rise of the East India Company. London: Bloomsbury.
Bayly, C.A. (1988). Indian Society and the Making of the British Empire. Cambridge University Press.

main_actor: Parisian sans-culottes and bourgeois militia
description: On 14 July 1789 a crowd of Parisian artisans and National Guard militia stormed the Bastille fortress-prison, killing the governor de Launay and seizing its arms and gunpowder. Symbolically marking the collapse of royal authority in Paris, the event triggered the abolition of feudal privileges in August and the Declaration of the Rights of Man (treated separately). It remains the founding moment of modern French republican identity.
casualties_source: Godechot, J. (1965). La prise de la Bastille (~83 insurgents and ~7 defenders killed)

sources.citation: Godechot, J. (1965). La prise de la Bastille, 14 juillet 1789. Paris: Gallimard.
Doyle, W. (2002). The Oxford History of the French Revolution. Oxford University Press.

entity_links.notes: republican trajectory initiated, First Republic proclaimed 1792

location_name: Southeastern United States to Indian Territory (Oklahoma)
main_actor: United States federal government under Presidents Andrew Jackson and Martin Van Buren; US Army under Generals Winfield Scott and others
description: Following the Indian Removal Act signed by Andrew Jackson in May 1830, the US federal government forcibly deported the Cherokee, Muscogee (Creek), Seminole, Chickasaw, and Choctaw nations from their ancestral lands east of the Mississippi to Indian Territory in present-day Oklahoma. An estimated 60,000 were deported; roughly a quarter died during the forced marches from exposure, starvation, and disease. The Cherokee removal of 1838-39 alone (Nu na da ul tsun yi, 'the trail where they cried') killed approximately 4,000 of 16,000 deportees.
casualties_source: Thornton, R. (1990). The Cherokee: A Population History (Cherokee deaths alone ~4,000-8,000; aggregate across five nations ~15,000-25,000)
ethical_notes: Classified ETHNIC_CLEANSING + DEPORTATION per ETHICS-007; the 19th-century US legal euphemism 'removal' is rejected. Main actor is the US federal government (not 'policy' or 'circumstances') because forced deportation was enacted by federal law, executed by federal troops, and carried out against the explicit 1832 Supreme Court ruling Worcester v. Georgia.

sources.citation: Thornton, R. (1990). The Cherokee: A Population History since 1500. University of Oklahoma Press.
Perdue, T. & Green, M.D. (2007). The Cherokee Nation and the Trail of Tears. Viking.
Indian Removal Act of 1830, 4 Stat. 411.

main_actor: British colonial administration of Van Diemen's Land, colonial settler militias (Vandemonian Roving Parties), and Lieutenant-Governor George Arthur (Black Line 1830)
description: Between the arrival of British penal colonists in 1803 and the death of Truganini in 1876, the indigenous population of Tasmania was reduced from an estimated 3,000-15,000 to zero full-descent survivors. Causes included settler-organised massacres (notably at Risdon 1804 and across the 'Black War' 1820s), the 1830 Black Line military sweep under Lt-Gov George Arthur, deliberate introduction of diseases, destruction of food sources, and the 1832 deportation of survivors to Flinders Island where most died of tuberculosis. Palawa community (mixed-descent descendants) continue to exist.
casualties_source: Ryan, L. (2012). Tasmanian Aborigines: A History since 1803
ethical_notes: Classified GENOCIDE per ETHICS-007, not 'decline', 'disappearance', or 'extinction' — these euphemisms erase the specific perpetrators. The British colonial administration is named as main actor because the Black Line of 1830 was an official military operation commanded by the Lieutenant-Governor. The framing 'the last Tasmanian died in 1876' is itself part of the erasure: Palawa descendants are present to this day.

sources.citation: Ryan, L. (2012). Tasmanian Aborigines: A History since 1803. Allen & Unwin.
Clements, N. (2014). The Black War: Fear, Sex and Resistance in Tasmania. University of Queensland Press.
Reynolds, H. (2001). An Indelible Stain? The Question of Genocide in Australia's History. Viking.

entity_links.notes: via British colonial administration; United Kingdom after 1801 Act of Union
specifically the Palawa peoples of lutruwita

location_name: Delhi, Kanpur, Lucknow, and northern India
main_actor: Sepoys of the Bengal Army, Mughal court (Bahadur Shah II), Rani Lakshmibai of Jhansi, Nana Sahib; suppressed by British East India Company forces and Crown reinforcements
description: A mutiny of sepoys at Meerut on 10 May 1857 rapidly widened into a broad anti-Company rebellion across northern and central India, encompassing peasant uprisings, principalities such as Jhansi and Awadh, and the restoration of the aged Mughal emperor Bahadur Shah II as nominal leader. The rebellion was suppressed over eighteen months with massive civilian reprisals — including the destruction of Delhi, hanging of thousands, and massacres at Allahabad, Kanpur, and Lucknow. Parliament responded by abolishing the East India Company in 1858 and transferring direct rule to the Crown.
casualties_source: Dalrymple, W. (2006). The Last Mughal (Delhi region alone; famine and disease deaths from 1857-59 famine in NW provinces remain contested, some estimates ~10M)
ethical_notes: Described as 'Sepoy Mutiny' in British sources, 'First War of Independence' in Indian nationalist historiography, and more neutrally as the Indian Rebellion of 1857. Included here as REBELLION per EventType enum. The scale of civilian reprisals is underrepresented in 19th-century sources; the figures in the Delhi sack alone are drawn from Dalrymple's Urdu-source research.

sources.citation: Dalrymple, W. (2006). The Last Mughal: The Fall of a Dynasty, Delhi 1857. Bloomsbury.
Roy, T. (2014). The Politics of a Popular Uprising: Bundelkhand in 1857. Oxford University Press.

entity_links.notes: formal end of the Mughal Sultanate

main_actor: British Royal Navy and forces of the Crown under Lord Palmerston (Prime Minister during phase II)
description: After Qing commissioner Lin Zexu seized and destroyed British opium stocks at Canton in 1839, Britain launched a naval war to force China to legalise the opium trade (British-controlled Bengal opium being the principal source) and open ports to British merchants. The Treaty of Nanjing (1842) ceded Hong Kong Island, opened five treaty ports, imposed an indemnity of 21 million silver dollars, and established extraterritoriality for British subjects. It is conventionally dated as the beginning of the Chinese 'century of humiliation'.
casualties_source: Platt, S. (2018). Imperial Twilight (Chinese military and civilian deaths)
ethical_notes: Classified CONQUEST because the operational goal and outcome was territorial and juridical seizure (Hong Kong, treaty ports, extraterritoriality). The moral asymmetry — a drug-smuggling war waged to protect narcotics commerce — is often softened in British textbooks as a 'trade war'.

sources.citation: Platt, S.R. (2018). Imperial Twilight: The Opium War and the End of China's Last Golden Age. Knopf.
Fay, P.W. (1975). The Opium War, 1840-1842. University of North Carolina Press.

entity_links.notes: United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland
territorial successor; actual belligerent was 大清 Qing Empire

main_actor: United Kingdom government under Sir Robert Peel and, crucially, Lord John Russell (laissez-faire policy under Treasury official Charles Trevelyan)
description: Phytophthora infestans destroyed the Irish potato crop of 1845-49, but the resulting mortality was catastrophic because of policy: UK government continued Ireland's grain exports, rejected further federal relief after 1847 as a matter of ideology, and prosecuted evictions under the Encumbered Estates Act. Approximately one million died of starvation and disease out of a population of eight million; another million emigrated. Ireland's population did not recover to pre-famine levels in the twentieth century.
casualties_source: Ó Gráda, C. (1999). Black '47 and Beyond: The Great Irish Famine
ethical_notes: Classified FAMINE with the UK government named as main actor because policy causation is documented in Parliamentary records: food exports continued, poor-law relief was placed on Irish landlords, and Trevelyan treated the famine as a providential corrective. British historiography long treated the famine as a natural disaster; Irish historiography from Mitchel (1861) forward has framed it as policy-driven.

sources.citation: Ó Gráda, C. (1999). Black '47 and Beyond: The Great Irish Famine in History, Economy, and Memory. Princeton University Press.
Kinealy, C. (1994). This Great Calamity: The Irish Famine 1845-52. Gill & Macmillan.
Mitchel, J. (1861). The Last Conquest of Ireland (Perhaps).

entity_links.notes: United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland

location_name: Northwest Caucasus and Black Sea coast
main_actor: Russian Empire Caucasus Army under General Nikolai Evdokimov, on orders of Alexander II
description: At the conclusion of the Russo-Circassian War, the Russian Empire carried out the systematic expulsion and mass killing of the Circassian (Adyghe) population of the northwestern Caucasus. Of an estimated 1-1.5 million Circassians, 600,000-1,500,000 died of massacre, starvation, and forced embarkation; survivors were deported to the Ottoman Empire in conditions that killed a further large fraction at sea. On 21 May 1864 the final Russian military ceremony at Kbaada (Krasnaya Polyana — site of the 2014 Sochi Olympic opening) marked official conclusion. The Circassian homeland was depopulated and the coastal population replaced with Russian settlers.
casualties_source: Richmond, W. (2013). The Circassian Genocide
silence_reason: Russian and Soviet state archives on the expulsion remain largely closed to independent researchers; the event has never been officially acknowledged by the Russian Federation (the Duma in 2014 explicitly refused a Circassian-parliament request for recognition). The 2014 Sochi Winter Olympics were held on the ground of the 1864 Kbaada final parade — widely interpreted by Circassian diaspora organisations as denial. Circassian oral tradition, Ottoman Ministry records in Istanbul, and a small body of Russian military correspondence are the main sources.
ethical_notes: Recognised as genocide by Georgia (2011) and by a growing body of scholarship (Shenfield 1999, Richmond 2013). Russian and much Western historiography still frame it as 'migration' or 'resettlement' — these are euphemisms per ETHICS-007.

sources.citation: Richmond, W. (2013). The Circassian Genocide. Rutgers University Press.
Shenfield, S. (1999). 'The Circassians: A Forgotten Genocide?' in Levene & Roberts, The Massacre in History.

description: The eruption of Krakatau on 27 August 1883 was one of the most energetic volcanic events in recorded history. The paroxysmal explosion was audible 4,800 km away at Rodrigues Island; pressure waves circled the globe multiple times; pyroclastic density currents and tsunamis reaching 30+ metres killed at least 36,000 people along the coasts of Java and Sumatra. Global average temperatures dropped approximately 1.2 degrees Celsius the following year; optical effects (red sunsets) were observed worldwide for up to three years.
casualties_source: Simkin, T. & Fiske, R.S. (1983). Krakatau 1883 (colonial census ~36,417; Javanese-language oral figures substantially higher)
ethical_notes: The official Dutch colonial casualty figure of 36,417 rests on undercounting of coastal Javanese populations; revisionist estimates based on oral tradition run substantially higher. Recorded as uncertainty in casualty range.

sources.citation: Simkin, T. & Fiske, R.S. (1983). Krakatau 1883: The Volcanic Eruption and Its Effects. Smithsonian Institution Press.
Winchester, S. (2003). Krakatoa: The Day the World Exploded. HarperCollins.

main_actor: King Leopold II of Belgium as personal sovereign; Force Publique and concessionary company agents (ABIR, Anversoise)
description: For twenty-three years King Leopold II of Belgium ruled the Congo Free State as his private property. To extract rubber and ivory the Force Publique and concessionary companies imposed village-level quotas enforced by hostage-taking of women, hand-severing of children for failed quotas, mass floggings with the chicotte, and wholesale village destruction. Demographer Jan Vansina estimated population loss of roughly 10 million — approximately half the pre-conquest population — through killing, starvation, exhaustion, and collapse of reproduction. In 1908 international pressure forced Leopold to transfer control to the Belgian state.
casualties_source: Vansina, J. in Hochschild (1998). King Leopold's Ghost; also Nzongola-Ntalaja (2002)
silence_reason: On transfer of sovereignty from Leopold to Belgium in 1908, large quantities of EIC (État Indépendant du Congo) archives were burnt at Leopold's direction. Belgian state historiography until the 1990s downplayed Congolese mortality. The 1904 Casement Report (British) and 1905 Belgian Commission provided the main contemporary documentation; Congolese oral testimony (the Kuba and Mongo testimonies collected by Vansina) was excluded from metropolitan sources for most of the twentieth century.
ethical_notes: Classified COLONIAL_VIOLENCE per ETHICS-007; death toll 8-10M. Leopold II named explicitly as main actor because the state was constitutionally his personal property 1885-1908. The Belgian government's 2020 statement of 'profound regret' remains short of recognising genocide.

sources.citation: Hochschild, A. (1998). King Leopold's Ghost: A Story of Greed, Terror, and Heroism in Colonial Africa. Houghton Mifflin.
Nzongola-Ntalaja, G. (2002). The Congo from Leopold to Kabila: A People's History. Zed Books.
Casement, R. (1904). Report on the Administration of the Congo Free State. UK Parliamentary Papers.

main_actor: Spanish Captain-General Valeriano Weyler y Nicolau, on orders of the Spanish government
description: To deny support to Cuban independence fighters Captain-General Weyler ordered the rural population of the western provinces forcibly concentrated into fortified towns (reconcentrados) starting October 1896. Inadequate food, water, sanitation, and shelter produced mass mortality from starvation and disease; contemporary US estimates put deaths between 150,000 and 400,000 out of a rural population of roughly 1.5 million. It is among the earliest industrial-scale civilian concentration policies; British and US observers applied the model in South Africa (1900-02) and in the Philippines (1901-02).
casualties_source: Tone, J. (2006). War and Genocide in Cuba 1895-1898
silence_reason: Spanish colonial archives in Havana were partially destroyed or removed on evacuation in 1898; US occupation records treated the dead as 'casualties of Spanish misgovernment' without independent audit. Cuban census figures for 1899 relative to 1887 show the population deficit but the geographic and ethnic distribution of losses is reconstructed rather than directly recorded.
ethical_notes: The Weyler reconcentration is the direct conceptual predecessor of the British concentration camps in the Second Boer War and of the US reconcentrados in the Philippines. Classified COLONIAL_VIOLENCE.

sources.citation: Tone, J.L. (2006). War and Genocide in Cuba, 1895-1898. University of North Carolina Press.
Pérez, L.A. (1998). The War of 1898: The United States and Cuba in History and Historiography. University of North Carolina Press.

main_actor: United States Army under Generals Elwell Otis, Arthur MacArthur, Jacob Smith (Samar); civilian administration under William Howard Taft
description: Following the 1898 US purchase of the Philippines from Spain, Filipino forces under Emilio Aguinaldo — who had already declared independence — fought a three-year war of resistance. US tactics included reconcentration on the Weyler model, particularly in Batangas and Samar. On Samar in 1901 Gen. Jacob Smith ordered 'kill everyone over ten' and 'make Samar a howling wilderness' (documented in his court-martial). The Moro campaigns on Mindanao and Sulu extended through 1913; the Bud Dajo massacre of March 1906 killed an estimated 800-1,000 Moro civilians sheltering in a volcanic crater. Filipino civilian deaths are most plausibly 200,000-600,000.
casualties_source: Gates, J. (1973). Schoolbooks and Krags; May (1991); Kramer (2006). The Blood of Government
silence_reason: US army records of the pacification campaigns were heavily sanitised before archiving; congressional inquiries (the Lodge Committee 1902) were partly held in executive session with classified transcripts released only in the 1970s. Filipino casualty figures rely on reconstruction from 1903 census anomalies and from Spanish-language local histories that US-trained Filipino historiography of the 1920s-50s tended to discount. The Bud Dajo and Bud Bagsak events remain absent from most US textbooks.
ethical_notes: Termed 'Philippine Insurrection' in US military usage until 1999 (renamed 'Philippine-American War' by Library of Congress). Classified COLONIAL_VIOLENCE per ETHICS-007. Main actor named in active voice.

sources.citation: Kramer, P.A. (2006). The Blood of Government: Race, Empire, the United States, and the Philippines. University of North Carolina Press.
Gates, J.M. (1973). Schoolbooks and Krags: The United States Army in the Philippines, 1898-1902. Greenwood.

main_actor: Satsuma-Chōshū alliance (Saigō Takamori, Ōkubo Toshimichi, Kido Takayoshi, Itō Hirobumi); Emperor Meiji as figurehead
description: The Boshin War (1868-69) restored imperial authority under Emperor Meiji, ending the Tokugawa shogunate. The oligarchy of the Satsuma-Chōshū alliance abolished the han domain system (1871), dissolved the samurai class (1873-76), established conscription and land-tax reform, promulgated the Meiji Constitution (1889), and pursued accelerated industrialisation under the slogan fukoku kyōhei ('rich country, strong army'). Japan thereby became the first non-Western state to reach industrial and military parity with European empires — and began its own imperial project in Taiwan (1895), Korea (annexed 1910), and Manchuria.
casualties_source: Jansen, M. (2000). The Making of Modern Japan (Boshin War military deaths)

sources.citation: Jansen, M. (2000). The Making of Modern Japan. Belknap/Harvard University Press.
Gordon, A. (2003). A Modern History of Japan. Oxford University Press.

main_actor: Bolshevik (RSDLP-B) party under Vladimir Lenin and Leon Trotsky; Military Revolutionary Committee of the Petrograd Soviet
description: On the night of 24-25 October 1917 (Julian calendar; 6-7 November Gregorian), Bolshevik Red Guards and sympathetic Petrograd garrison units seized the Winter Palace, the bridges, and communications. The Provisional Government was deposed; the Second Congress of Soviets ratified the transfer of power. The Russian Civil War (1918-22) that followed produced 7-12 million deaths. The Soviet state established here would shape twentieth-century global politics until its dissolution in 1991.
casualties_source: Figes, O. (1996). A People's Tragedy (low immediate fatalities in Petrograd seizure; civil war deaths counted separately)
ethical_notes: The October Revolution itself produced few immediate deaths in Petrograd; the historic weight lies in the civil war and the Soviet state that followed. The Russian state under Putin officially reclassifies 1917 as a 'catastrophe' rather than a revolution; Soviet-era hagiographic accounts in the opposite direction are equally unreliable. The date convention used here is Julian as per contemporary usage.

sources.citation: Figes, O. (1996). A People's Tragedy: The Russian Revolution 1891-1924. Jonathan Cape.
Rabinowitch, A. (2007). The Bolsheviks in Power: The First Year of Soviet Rule in Petrograd. Indiana University Press.

name_original: 広島・長崎への原子爆弾投下
main_actor: United States government under President Harry S. Truman; USAAF 509th Composite Group
description: On 6 August 1945 the USAAF dropped a uranium-gun device ('Little Boy') on Hiroshima from the B-29 Enola Gay; on 9 August a plutonium-implosion device ('Fat Man') was dropped on Nagasaki from Bockscar. Immediate deaths are estimated at 70,000-80,000 at Hiroshima and 40,000 at Nagasaki; by the end of 1945 the combined toll exceeded 200,000 from burns, blast injuries, and acute radiation syndrome. Excess cancer mortality among survivors (hibakusha) continued across decades. The two bombings remain the only military uses of nuclear weapons in history.
casualties_source: Committee for the Compilation of Materials on Damage Caused by the Atomic Bombs (1981); RERF longitudinal study
ethical_notes: Classified MASSACRE per ETHICS-007 because civilian targets were selected; also has TECHNOLOGICAL_EVENT character for the first operational use of nuclear fission weapons. US historiography remains divided on military necessity; Japanese sources (hibakusha testimony, municipal and RERF records) foreground civilian targeting and radiation effects. Main actor is named in active voice including explicit presidential authorisation.

sources.citation: Committee for the Compilation of Materials on Damage Caused by the Atomic Bombs in Hiroshima and Nagasaki (1981). Hiroshima and Nagasaki: The Physical, Medical, and Social Effects of the Atomic Bombings. Basic Books.
Hasegawa, T. (2005). Racing the Enemy: Stalin, Truman, and the Surrender of Japan. Harvard University Press.
Radiation Effects Research Foundation. Life Span Study cohort data.

main_actor: People's Republic of China leadership under Mao Zedong; Chinese Communist Party Politburo Standing Committee
description: The Great Leap Forward (1958-62) imposed forced agricultural collectivisation, mass requisition of village grain, impossible production quotas, and the diversion of peasant labour into backyard-furnace steel production. The result was the largest famine in recorded history: excess mortality estimates converge on 30-45 million between 1959 and 1961. Grain was exported during the famine peak; internal dissent by Peng Dehuai at Lushan (1959) was denounced as right-deviationism. The famine was officially denied under the term sannian ziran zaihai ('Three Years of Natural Disasters') until at least the 1980s.
casualties_source: Dikötter, F. (2010). Mao's Great Famine; Yang Jisheng (2008/2012). Tombstone
silence_reason: PRC state archives on the 1959-61 famine remain partially classified; the euphemism sannian ziran zaihai ('Three Years of Natural Disasters') was official state framing into the 1980s and remains current in popular usage. Yang Jisheng (former Xinhua journalist) accessed provincial archives in the 1990s-2000s and documented the figures; his work Tombstone is banned in mainland China.
ethical_notes: Classified FAMINE per ETHICS-007, not 'three difficult years' or 'natural disasters'. Main actor named explicitly; the causal chain from collectivisation, to grain requisition, to continued exports during mass starvation, is documented in provincial records accessed by Yang Jisheng and Dikötter.

sources.citation: Yang Jisheng (2012, English trans.). Tombstone: The Great Chinese Famine, 1958-1962. Farrar, Straus and Giroux.
Dikötter, F. (2010). Mao's Great Famine: The History of China's Most Devastating Catastrophe, 1958-62. Bloomsbury.
Becker, J. (1996). Hungry Ghosts: Mao's Secret Famine. Free Press.

main_actor: Indonesian Army under Major General Suharto; allied Islamic militias (Ansor, Banser); with operational intelligence support from the US CIA and UK Foreign Office
description: Following the alleged 30 September 1965 coup attempt (Gerakan 30 September) the Indonesian Army under Suharto carried out the systematic killing of suspected members and sympathisers of the Communist Party of Indonesia (PKI), ethnic Chinese, trade unionists, teachers, and rural reformers. Conservative academic estimates of those killed in six months range from 500,000 to over 1,000,000; the US Embassy supplied the Indonesian Army with lists of PKI cadres (the 'Palmer List'). The killings cleared the path for Suharto's New Order (1967-1998); none of the perpetrators were ever tried.
casualties_source: Cribb, R. (ed.) (1990). The Indonesian Killings; Robinson, G. (2018). The Killing Season
silence_reason: New Order state archives remain substantially closed; the Indonesian state has never carried out a truth-and-reconciliation process and the 2012 Komnas HAM findings were suppressed. US diplomatic cables from Jakarta (partially released via National Security Archive FOIA) document the Embassy providing names to the Indonesian Army. UK Foreign Office propaganda operations (IRD) remained classified until the 2010s. The 2015 presidential apology proposal was blocked.
ethical_notes: Classified GENOCIDE per ETHICS-007 recognition and per the 2016 International People's Tribunal finding (non-binding). The ethnicity component — targeted killing of ethnic Chinese — meets the UN Genocide Convention definition; the political-group component (PKI) does not under the Convention but is often classed as 'politicide'. Main actor explicitly named with Western complicity.

sources.citation: Robinson, G. (2018). The Killing Season: A History of the Indonesian Massacres, 1965-66. Princeton University Press.
Melvin, J. (2018). The Army and the Indonesian Genocide. Routledge.
National Security Archive (George Washington University). Indonesia 1965 cable collection.

entity_links.notes: CIA operational support and names provision

main_actor: Indonesian Armed Forces (ABRI/TNI) under Suharto (1975-98) and Habibie (1998-99); with diplomatic and military-aid support from the United States, United Kingdom, and Australia
description: On 7 December 1975 Indonesian armed forces invaded East Timor, nine days after the Portuguese decolonisation and FRETILIN's unilateral declaration of independence. During 24 years of occupation an estimated 102,800-183,000 East Timorese died — roughly one-quarter of the pre-invasion population — from killing, forced starvation (notably the 1979 'fence of legs' counterinsurgency), and disease in resettlement camps. Following the 30 August 1999 independence referendum, Indonesian-backed militias destroyed approximately 70% of infrastructure and killed a further 1,400-2,000.
casualties_source: Commission for Reception, Truth and Reconciliation in East Timor (CAVR, 2005). Chega! Report
silence_reason: Indonesian military records for the occupation period were substantially destroyed or remain classified. The 2005 CAVR Chega! Report was formally submitted to the UN Security Council but its implementation recommendations — including reparations from Indonesia and its foreign backers — have not been pursued; the report is not widely disseminated in Indonesia. US and Australian diplomatic records from the crucial December 1975 period were partially released only after decades (the 'Ford-Kissinger cable' of 6 December 1975 confirming US green light was declassified in 2001).
ethical_notes: The CAVR specifically found that the Indonesian military committed acts of extermination, enslavement, deportation, torture, rape, and persecution constituting crimes against humanity; characterisation as genocide rests on the proportion of population killed and on specific targeting patterns. Main actor explicitly named with foreign complicity.

sources.citation: Commission for Reception, Truth and Reconciliation in East Timor (2005). Chega! Final Report. Dili.
Kiernan, B. (2003). 'The Demography of Genocide in Southeast Asia'. Critical Asian Studies 35(4).

name_original: ការ​ឃាត​ប្រល័យ​ពូជ​សាសន៍​ខ្មែរ
main_actor: Khmer Rouge regime (Communist Party of Kampuchea) under Pol Pot (Saloth Sâr); Nuon Chea, Ieng Sary, Khieu Samphan; organs of Santebal (S-21) under Kaing Guek Eav (Duch)
description: From the fall of Phnom Penh on 17 April 1975 until the Vietnamese invasion in January 1979, the Khmer Rouge regime carried out the systematic killing of perceived enemies, urban populations, ethnic and religious minorities (Cham Muslims, ethnic Chinese, ethnic Vietnamese), Buddhist clergy, and Eastern Zone cadre. Demographic reconstruction converges on 1.5-2.0 million deaths — roughly a quarter of the population — from execution, starvation, forced labour, and untreated disease. The S-21 security facility alone processed approximately 15,000 prisoners, of whom 7 are known to have survived. The Cham and ethnic Vietnamese components meet the UN Genocide Convention definition; the broader class-war killings are conventionally termed 'politicide' or 'auto-genocide'.
casualties_source: Kiernan, B. (2008). The Pol Pot Regime; Documentation Center of Cambodia; ECCC judgments
ethical_notes: The Extraordinary Chambers in the Courts of Cambodia (ECCC) convicted Nuon Chea and Khieu Samphan of genocide (against Cham and ethnic Vietnamese) in 2018; broader convictions for crimes against humanity include the non-minority killings. Pol Pot died in 1998 without trial. China and the United States continued to recognise Democratic Kampuchea's UN seat until 1993.

sources.citation: Kiernan, B. (2008). The Pol Pot Regime: Race, Power, and Genocide in Cambodia under the Khmer Rouge, 1975-79 (3rd ed.). Yale University Press.
Extraordinary Chambers in the Courts of Cambodia. Case 002/02 Judgment (2018).
Chandler, D. (1999). Voices from S-21: Terror and History in Pol Pot's Secret Prison. University of California Press.

entity_links.entity_name_original: សាធារណរដ្ឋខ្មែរ
entity_links.notes: predecessor Khmer Republic; Democratic Kampuchea was the formal state name

entity_links.entity_name_original: ព្រះរាជាណាចក្រកម្ពុជា

main_actor: Guatemalan Army under Generals Romeo Lucas García (1978-82) and Efraín Ríos Montt (1982-83); with US CIA training, funding, and materiel support
description: During the peak phase of the Guatemalan Civil War, the army carried out a systematic counterinsurgency against rural Maya populations suspected of sympathy with the URNG guerrillas. The Commission for Historical Clarification (CEH, 1999) documented 626 massacres and the destruction of 400+ villages in the Ixil, Q'anjob'al, and K'iche' Maya regions. Approximately 200,000 Guatemalans died or were disappeared in the broader 1960-96 conflict, of whom the CEH attributes 93% of violations to state forces and allied paramilitaries. In 2013 Ríos Montt was convicted of genocide against the Ixil Maya by a Guatemalan court; the verdict was annulled ten days later on procedural grounds.
casualties_source: Commission for Historical Clarification / CEH (1999). Guatemala: Memory of Silence
silence_reason: Guatemalan military archives of Plan Victoria 82 and Plan Sofía were partially surrendered to the CEH in 1999 but substantial portions remain missing; the Archivo Histórico de la Policía Nacional discovered in 2005 filled significant gaps but the armed-forces archives remain largely sealed. US CIA records on the Reagan-era support have been only partially declassified. Ríos Montt's 2013 genocide conviction was annulled and he died in 2018 before retrial; a final Guatemalan-state acknowledgement of genocide has not been issued.
ethical_notes: The CEH explicitly found acts of genocide under the UN Convention definition against four Maya groups. The 'Silent Holocaust' term is Cahuec-Montt's rendering. Main actor is named with explicit US support per CIA records released through the National Security Archive.

sources.citation: Commission for Historical Clarification (CEH) (1999). Guatemala: Memoria del Silencio. UN/Guatemala.
Sanford, V. (2003). Buried Secrets: Truth and Human Rights in Guatemala. Palgrave.
Schirmer, J. (1998). The Guatemalan Military Project: A Violence Called Democracy. University of Pennsylvania Press.

name_original: Masacră la Srebrenica / Геноцид у Сребреници
main_actor: Army of Republika Srpska (VRS) under General Ratko Mladić; paramilitary Scorpions; with complicity of Radovan Karadžić's civilian leadership
description: Between 11 and 22 July 1995 Bosnian Serb forces under General Mladić overran the UN-declared 'safe area' of Srebrenica (garrisoned by a lightly armed Dutch UNPROFOR battalion) and systematically executed 8,372 identified Bosniak men and boys aged roughly 12-77. The bodies were buried, then disinterred by bulldozer weeks later and reburied in scattered secondary graves to conceal the crime — a feature unique among modern genocides that has enabled DNA-based identification across decades by the International Commission on Missing Persons. The ICJ (2007) and the ICTY/MICT (Mladić conviction 2017, Karadžić 2016) ruled the event genocide under the UN Genocide Convention.
casualties_source: International Commission on Missing Persons; ICTY Prosecutor v. Krstić (2001)
ethical_notes: Classified GENOCIDE per ETHICS-007 per ICJ and ICTY rulings. Republika Srpska's own 2004 Srebrenica Commission confirmed the facts; subsequent RS leadership has oscillated between acknowledgement and denial — current Bosnian Serb leadership (2023-24) has withdrawn from the 2004 report. The Dutch UNPROFOR and UN command failures are secondary but documented in the 1999 UN Secretary-General's Report.

sources.citation: ICTY. Prosecutor v. Krstić (IT-98-33), Trial Judgment (2001) and Appeal Judgment (2004).
ICJ. Application of the Convention on the Prevention and Punishment of the Crime of Genocide (Bosnia v. Serbia) Judgment (2007).
Honig, J.W. & Both, N. (1996). Srebrenica: Record of a War Crime. Penguin.

main_actor: NKVD of the USSR, executing Politburo decision No. P13/144 of 5 March 1940 signed by Stalin, Voroshilov, Molotov, and Mikoyan
description: Between April and May 1940 the Soviet NKVD executed approximately 22,000 Polish citizens — officers, police, border guards, intellectuals, and professionals — taken prisoner after the 1939 Soviet invasion of eastern Poland. The killings were carried out at Katyn Forest (4,421 victims), Kalinin NKVD prison (6,311), Kharkiv NKVD (3,820), and other sites. The USSR denied responsibility from 1943 (when Wehrmacht forces discovered mass graves at Katyn) until 1990, when Mikhail Gorbachev formally acknowledged NKVD responsibility and released some documents. The signed Politburo decision was released in 1992 by Yeltsin.
casualties_source: Official NKVD accounting in 1959 Shelepin memorandum (21,857); Polish IPN investigation
silence_reason: Soviet denial from 1943 to 1990 was maintained across seven general secretaries; the 1944 Burdenko Commission falsely attributed the killings to German forces. Numbering approximately 22,000 executions the event was one of the best-documented silences of the twentieth century: signed Politburo documents existed throughout but were held in the Presidential (former CPSU) archive. Polish-language scholarship (Madajczyk, Zawodny) reconstructed the event from German-occupation exhumations and from the 1959 Shelepin memorandum only after partial 1990-92 declassification. Contemporary Russian state education under Putin has again begun to minimise Soviet responsibility.
ethical_notes: Classified MASSACRE per ETHICS-007. Polish scholarship and the Polish state have pursued genocide classification; Russia rejects this. The 2010 Smolensk air disaster killed much of the Polish state leadership while flying to the Katyn commemoration. Primary sources here are the Politburo protocol signed by Stalin and the 1959 Shelepin memorandum requesting destruction of the files (preserved precisely because it had not been acted on).

sources.citation: Cienciala, A., Lebedeva, N., & Materski, W. (eds) (2007). Katyn: A Crime Without Punishment. Yale University Press.
Politburo of the CPSU Central Committee, Protocol P13/144 of 5 March 1940. Russian State Archive of Socio-Political History.
Shelepin, A. Memorandum to Khrushchev, 3 March 1959. Declassified 1992.

main_actor: Tim Berners-Lee (CERN); with Robert Cailliau (engineering partner)
description: In March 1989 Tim Berners-Lee submitted 'Information Management: A Proposal' to his supervisor Mike Sendall at CERN, sketching what would become the World Wide Web. By December 1990 the first HTTP server (info.cern.ch), first browser (WorldWideWeb), and first web page were operational on a NeXT workstation. On 30 April 1993 CERN placed the WWW source code in the public domain. The Web would become the foundational communications substrate of the twenty-first century, transforming scientific publishing, commerce, politics, and warfare. The choice of royalty-free open specifications is a principal reason for its universality.
ethical_notes: The WWW is often conflated with the internet (ARPANET 1969, TCP/IP 1983). Recorded here as TECHNOLOGICAL_EVENT distinct from the underlying network.

sources.citation: Berners-Lee, T. (1989). Information Management: A Proposal. CERN internal circulation.
Berners-Lee, T. (1999). Weaving the Web. HarperSanFrancisco.
Gillies, J. & Cailliau, R. (2000). How the Web Was Born. Oxford University Press.

location_name: Indian Ocean, epicentre off Aceh, Sumatra
description: On 26 December 2004 a magnitude 9.1-9.3 megathrust earthquake ruptured the Sunda subduction zone off northwestern Sumatra, lifting the sea floor along a 1,200-1,600 km fault segment. The resulting tsunami reached 30+ metres on the Aceh coast of Indonesia and propagated across the Indian Ocean, killing at least 227,898 people across fourteen countries; Indonesia (168,000+), Sri Lanka (35,000+), India (18,000+), and Thailand (8,200+) sustained the worst mortality. The event exposed the absence of an Indian Ocean tsunami warning system and triggered the 2006 establishment of IOTWS.
casualties_source: USGS; national disaster management agencies; Indonesia's BRR reported 167,000+ in Aceh alone
ethical_notes: Casualty figures for Aceh in particular were initially undercounted because of prior conflict conditions (GAM insurgency) that had restricted journalistic and census access. Indonesian official figure of 167,799 is widely cited; true Aceh toll may exceed 200,000.

sources.citation: Lay, T. et al. (2005). 'The great Sumatra-Andaman earthquake of 26 December 2004'. Science 308: 1127-1133.
Athukorala, P. & Resosudarmo, B. (2005). 'The Indian Ocean Tsunami: Economic Impact, Disaster Management and Lessons'. Asian Economic Papers 4(1).
